text: Existing tunnels may undergo deformation due to the unloading effects from adjacent excavation pits, which can impact the normal operation and safety of the tunnels, making deformation control crucial. Based on extensive engineering case studies and actual measurement data from a deep pit adjacent to an existing tunnel in Tianjin, a finite element method considering the small-strain stiffness characteristics of soil was employed to analyze the deformation impact of pit construction on existing tunnels outside the pit. By integrating various deformation control standards from different codes, different deformation patterns of support structures were categorized, along with the regions of deformation impact on existing tunnels under maximum horizontal displacement conditions. Research findings indicate that the deformation influence zone outside the pit can generally be simplified into a right trapezoidal shape. Based on the potential deformation patterns, maximum deformation, and the relative position of the tunnel to the excavation pit arising from the actual engineering pit support structures, the influence zone can be utilized to estimate the potential deformation of the tunnel. Under the same conditions of deformation mode and control values for the support structures, the extent of the deformation influence zone increases as the maximum horizontal displacement of the support structure rises; under equal maximum horizontal displacement and deformation control values, the deformation influence zone is smallest for cantilever-type deformation patterns, followed by convex-type and composite types, with the base type exhibiting the largest zone.

A total of 35 foundation pit engineering cases with wall-back surface settlement observation data were collected from the Shanghai region. From a statistical perspective, the characteristics of surface deformation behind deep foundation pits in Shanghai's soft soil area were explored, leading to the following conclusions: 

(1) The maximum surface settlement shows an increasing trend with the increase in excavation depth, generally ranging between 0.1%H and 0.8%H, with an average value of 0.38%H, and it was compared with statistical results from other regions worldwide. 

(2) Factors affecting the maximum surface settlement were studied, and the results indicated that maximum surface settlement increases with the thickness of the soft soil layer behind the wall and decreases with the increase in the uplift stability factor at the bottom of the pit, while there is little correlation with the wall embedded ratio and the stiffness of the support system.

(3) The ratio of maximum surface settlement to maximum wall deflection generally ranges from 0.4 to 2.0, with an average value of about 0.84. This is closely aligned with the patterns observed in soft soil foundation pits as recorded by Moormann [111].

(4) The distribution pattern of surface settlement behind the wall was statistically analyzed, providing an envelope curve for the settlement, which was compared with relevant scholars' research findings. For foundation pits with a trough-shaped surface settlement distribution, the surface settlement close to the retaining wall generally ranges from 0 to 0.5%H, while the maximum surface settlement typically occurs within a distance of 0.3H to 1.0H from the retaining wall, and diminishes to negligible values within a range of 1.0H to 4.0H.

(5) The maximum surface tilt ranges from 0.001 to 0.017, and a statistical relationship for predicting maximum surface tilt based on maximum surface settlement was presented, which can be used for preliminary assessment of the impact of foundation pit excavation on adjacent structures.

Taking the excavation project of a foundation pit near a metro tunnel in Shanghai as the backdrop, a three-dimensional numerical analysis model was established using FLAC-3D software to dynamically simulate the entire construction process of the foundation pit. The results indicate that the computed results are generally consistent with the engineering monitoring data, and the excavation of the foundation pit on one side can cause asymmetric deformation of the tunnel structure. To protect the adjacent tunnel, a new construction technique of secondary reinforcement outside the pit was proposed and implemented. It was highlighted for the first time that the foundation reinforcement bodies and underground structures, considered as heterogeneous materials, have a mitigating effect on the displacement transfer caused by nearby foundation pit excavations. Numerical simulations of different construction schemes were conducted, and comparative analysis demonstrated that secondary reinforcement of the soil adjacent to the underground continuous wall of the foundation pit, along with reverse construction of the structure, can effectively control the deformation of the neighboring tunnel.

With the expansion of underground space development, excavation projects are advancing towards greater depths and larger scales. This study examines and summarizes the measured results from 46 successful deep excavation sites in the soft soil regions of Hangzhou and Shanghai. It analyzes the relationship between excavation depth and maximum lateral displacement and its location, as well as the correlation between excavation depth and the relative stiffness of the supporting systems currently employed. Given the close relationship between the stability safety factor against uplift and excavation deformation, this paper analyzes the measured data and establishes the relationship between the safety factor against uplift stability and the maximum lateral displacement of the retaining structure, as well as the maximum ground settlement behind the wall. Furthermore, it presents viewpoints on the deformation control standards for deep excavations as depth increases. The analysis of the measured data leads to the following conclusions: as excavation depth increases, due to the rapid growth of relative stiffness of the supporting systems, the maximum relative lateral displacement does not increase significantly. The maximum displacement typically occurs near the excavation face, showing a tendency to move upwards with increasing depth. The enhancement of the uplift stability safety factor plays a crucial role in reducing deformation at the excavation site. Moreover, even when the deformation exceeds the current regulation control standards with increasing excavation depth, safety can be maintained for both the excavation itself and the surrounding environment.

This article analyzes the factors affecting the time-space effects of internal forces and deformations in deep excavation support structures, such as stepwise excavation depth, excavation width, boundary constraint conditions, and the rheological behavior of the foundation. The main conclusions obtained are as follows: 

1) When the support stiffness increases, the maximum lateral displacement of the wall decreases, while the maximum longitudinal bending moment Mx, transverse bending moment M, and torque Mxy increase. It is evident that to effectively control the lateral displacement of the wall without significantly enlarging the internal forces, the increase in support stiffness should be limited.

2) Within a certain range, appropriately increasing the thickness of the retaining wall can effectively reduce the lateral displacement of the wall; however, when a certain value is reached, the effect of increasing the wall thickness on displacement is minimal. Additionally, increasing wall thickness will increase project costs, so it is unreasonable to primarily rely on this method to reduce displacement when the wall strength meets the requirements.

3) Increasing the preloading axial force of the supports can effectively reduce the lateral displacement of the wall with little increase in internal forces; however, the preloading axial force of the first support must be appropriate, as excessive force could lead to wall tilting.

4) When the elastic modulus of the soil in the pit is increased to 20 times the original value, the lateral displacement of the wall is reduced by 36% compared to the original state, indicating that reinforcement of the passive zone soil significantly reduces wall lateral displacement. On the other hand, as the soil's elastic modulus increases, the internal forces in the wall decrease, showing that such measures are highly beneficial for enhancing the stability of the retaining wall.

5) Increasing the embedment depth of the wall can effectively reduce the lateral displacement; simultaneously, the positive bending moment Mx, M, and positive torque Mxy will decrease, while the negative bending moment and torque will slightly increase, which aids in maintaining wall stability.

6) The transverse bending moment My along the horizontal direction of the wall is small, whereas the vertical bending moment M along the depth direction is large, with the absolute value of positive bending moments far exceeding that of negative moments. Therefore, the design should primarily focus on the arrangement of vertical reinforcement.

7) Due to the constraints from adjacent walls at both ends, the lateral displacement of the wall is larger in the middle and smaller at the ends; in the depth direction, the lateral displacement is large in the middle with smaller values at the upper and lower ends. The contour map of the wall's lateral displacement approximates a "boat shape," with the maximum displacement occurring near the excavation surface.

8) As the width of the excavation increases, the displacement values of the wall increase; when the width of the excavation doubles, the maximum longitudinal bending moment M significantly increases. It is evident that in practical engineering, attention should be paid to avoid excessive excavation width to maintain wall stability.

9) Both wall displacement and internal forces increase over time, therefore for deep excavations in soft soil strata, the excavation and support procedures should be expedited rather than delayed.

Cracking and deformation of building foundations in red soil regions is a significant geological hazard, resulting in severe economic losses and social ramifications. This type of disaster is particularly prevalent in southwestern China, with a notable example being the Puzhi area in the western suburbs of Kunming. In this region, numerous two to three-story brick structures built on red soil slopes have experienced significant cracking and deformation of their foundations. In some areas, this has led to slope soil failure, resulting in soil slides and causing adverse social impacts. 

The root cause of foundation cracking and deformation in red soil areas lies in the process of laterization itself. The initial formation of blocky and granular red soil has poor stability, especially in soil slopes composed of these two types, which further exacerbates foundation cracking and deformation, leading to structural damage. In contrast, the typical red soil formed during the later stages of laterization exhibits relatively stable engineering geological properties, particularly due to its material foundation—structural units that possess high strength (undamaged under a pressure of 149 kg/cm²) and exhibit some degree of cementation between units, which usually translates to characteristics of high strength and low compressibility. 

However, geological engineering investigation standards and design practitioners in China often emphasize this advantage of red soil, neglecting its drawbacks such as water-absorption swelling and shrinkage cracking upon desiccation. Consequently, many construction projects have suffered damage. A typical case is observed in a project in Gejiu City, Yunnan, where the red soil foundation consistency was below zero, operating in a semi-dry state (60–70% saturation), leading to a design value of 1.7 kg/cm² according to relevant standards. Ultimately, this caused a maximum settlement of 10 cm and differential settlement of 5 cm. Field investigations and load tests revealed a proportional limit of only 1.3 kg/cm², primarily due to the development of shrinkage cracks within the red soil foundation, which compromised the structural integrity and strength of the foundation, posing a danger to the project.

Moreover, in the Puzhi area at the edge of the Kunming basin, deforestation, prolonged drought conditions, and a marked decline in groundwater levels (exceeding 4 m) have sharply reduced the moisture content within the red soil foundation (approximately 10–30% decrease). This has led to the emergence of shrinkage cracks, compounded by other adverse factors and additional loads from construction, resulting in severe cracking and deformation of the foundation soil. In localized areas, ground fissures have connected and penetrated the entire slope soil mass, leading to soil slides, with the affected area continuing to expand.

In response to the excessive deformation of the deep excavation in thick silty soft soil, a combination support scheme utilizing cement-soil mixing piles and underground diaphragm walls was adopted. To investigate the deformation characteristics of the diaphragm walls within this support scheme, FLAC3D numerical simulation software was utilized to analyze the deep foundation pit of the Shenzhen Metro Line 10 parking lot. The results indicate that the simulated values fit well with the measured data, predicting that the final settlement of the soil surrounding the excavation after completion will be 22.91 mm, which is below the alert value. Under the support conditions of cement-soil mixing piles, in addition to the original condition of a 1,200 mm thick diaphragm wall, the deformation scenarios for wall thicknesses of 1,100 mm, 1,000 mm, 900 mm, 800 mm, and 700 mm were simulated. During the first layer of excavation, the settlements among the various conditions were similar. As excavation proceeded, the settlement values began to change, with the minimum settlement of 47.33 mm occurring at the 1,100 mm thickness condition, while the maximum settlement reached 93.85 mm at the 700 mm thickness condition. The maximum horizontal deformation of the diaphragm wall occurred approximately 15 m from the top of the wall. Under the 700 mm condition, the maximum deformation reached 42.58 mm, while the minimum under the 1,100 mm condition was 25.71 mm. Therefore, for this deep foundation pit project under the slotting conditions of cement-soil mixing pile support, it is feasible to appropriately reduce the thickness of the diaphragm wall, with a 1,100 mm thick diaphragm wall ensuring safety while reducing costs.

In response to the issues of water leakage and deformation in foundation pits of water-rich sandy layers, this study uses a subway station foundation pit project as a backdrop. Numerical simulation and field measurement methods are employed to compare the lateral displacement of the foundation pit retaining wall, ground settlement behind the wall, and the internal force changes in the retaining pile wall before and after local leakage of the waterproof curtain in sandy soil conditions. The research results indicate that local leakage of the waterproof curtain exacerbates the impact of seepage on foundation pit deformation. The lateral displacement curve of the retaining pile evolves from a linear distribution to a bulging shape as the excavation depth increases. The lateral displacement curve of the deep soil behind the wall transitions from a nonlinear bulging shape to a linear distribution with increasing horizontal distance (Lp). Local leakage of the waterproof curtain also increases the ground settlement and its affected area, with the settlement after leakage expanding significantly to 2-3 times that before leakage. The internal forces within the retaining pile gradually increase with the excavation depth, while the maximum shear force and bending moment of the pile decrease after leakage compared to before. Controlling the expansion of seepage pathways and preventing increased soil and water loss are effective strategies to mitigate the worsening of foundation pit leakage hazards. The findings can provide valuable references for the prevention and management of leakage disasters in deep foundation pits in sandy areas.

In recent years, the construction of urban rail transit in coastal areas of China has rapidly developed. The characteristics of deep foundation pits for coastal rail transit include significant embedment depth of supportive structures, complex surrounding environments, intricate internal support construction processes, and variable hydrogeological parameters. Stability of the foundation pits and protection of the surrounding environment have become key issues in the design and construction of the pits, with deformation control playing a decisive role. The deformation of deep foundation pits for coastal rail transit is influenced by multiple factors, including geotechnical properties, external loads, groundwater conditions, and construction organization, representing a complex geotechnical engineering problem. Conducting monitoring research on deep foundation pit deformation is of significant value for the safe construction of urban rail transit, particularly when establishing a comprehensive automated health monitoring and early warning management technology that considers the specific characteristics of deep foundation pits in coastal areas. This paper builds upon the existing applications of monitoring and early warning technologies for deep foundation pits in some urban rail transit systems in the coastal area, integrating automated monitoring data assessment with a geotechnical engineering expert system to explore the application research of automated health monitoring technology for deep foundation pits in coastal rail transit projects. The paper details the methods and content of monitoring for deep foundation pit engineering in the coastal area, summarizes the types, characteristics, and causes of geological hazards associated with urban rail transit foundation pits, and introduces intelligent automated monitoring equipment to provide all-weather data collection, data transmission, and initial assessment of deformation data. It incorporates an intelligent expert evaluation system that conducts numerical comparisons and health assessments of foundation pits by integrating multiple factors such as geological conditions, hydrogeological conditions, support engineering design, and construction organization, thus providing diagnoses and early warnings regarding the health status of the pits. The expert system introduced in the application research effectively integrates geological conditions, hydrogeological parameters, and procedural organization in construction to achieve mutual verification of deformations and timely corrections of hydrogeological parameters, greatly guiding the safe construction of foundation pits. The outcomes of this application research provide technical support for the design, construction, deformation control, and health monitoring of deep foundation pits in coastal rail transit, offering practical references for the construction of deep foundation pit projects in urban rail transit within coastal regions.

For buildings, once excavation begins, the first issue encountered is the management of groundwater. In several plain cities of Henan Province, the terrain is typically characterized by alluvial plains, primarily composed of Quaternary Holocene sedimentary silt and silty clay, with a high water content. Additionally, due to the high groundwater level, piping often occurs immediately after the excavation of the foundation pit. Under hydrodynamic action, pressure is exerted on soil particles, leading to soil erosion, which then flows out through the gaps in the underground diaphragm wall formed by the foundation piles. Over time, this process results in the development of cracks and cavities, causing surface subsidence and subsequent settlement of the structure. This generally leads to uneven settlement of surrounding buildings, with greater subsidence occurring closer to the foundation pit. If neighboring structures are very close to the foundation pit, and considering that the construction site may encounter uncontrollable random factors—such as narrow site conditions, static load overload around the pit, dynamic loads (vehicles, construction machinery) triggering disturbance, and sudden heavy rainfall—the risk of destructive deformation increases significantly. This may cause surrounding buildings to tilt and crack, ultimately resulting in accidents.

The geological hazards induced by human activities, particularly those linked to urban construction, are becoming increasingly prominent. The Yellow River alluvial plain is a result of secondary transportation of loess, and the unique physical and mechanical properties of its geological materials make it difficult to directly apply existing construction practices from other regions. The deformation of building foundations and excavation pits has become a significant geological hazard. The soil behaves as a viscoelastic material, and the stress release caused by excavation often manifests as creep in a plastic manner, contributing to the deformation of building foundations and excavation pits. In the northern Henan Yellow River alluvial plain, the deformation of building foundations and excavation pits is largely due to construction issues but also significantly influenced by the lack of understanding of the characteristics of the relatively recent strata formed by the river's transportation and sorting of loess. In other areas, as long as the excavation remains above the water level, there are usually no major issues with excavation, and it can be calculated using retaining wall theory. However, in the Yellow River alluvial plain, this is often not the case—excavations do not commonly experience collapse, but rather surrounding structures undergo deformation. Additionally, some buildings that were constructed years ago continue to settle, which suggests that the local geological materials are exhibiting plastic behavior beyond our conventional understanding of soil behavior.

The July 20th heavy rain disaster in Zhengzhou triggered numerous foundation pit engineering accidents. In addressing the safety concerns of deep foundation pits for rail transit stations under extreme rainfall conditions, this study relies on actual engineering projects. By organizing and analyzing field data of lateral displacement of retaining structures, axial forces of steel bracing, and vertical displacement of underground pipelines, the safety of foundation pit support for rail transit stations is examined, and emergency response measures are proposed. 

Research findings indicate that the impact of the rainstorm disaster led to a rapid increase in horizontal displacement of the pile bodies, with the axial force change curve of the steel bracing exhibiting a characteristic "C" shape over time, showing a sharp rise in axial force, exceeding design values at its peak. Significant vertical displacement was observed in pipelines surrounding the foundation pit, indicating risks to pit safety. 

In emergency responses under extreme rainfall conditions, it is essential to increase monitoring frequency, seal water ingress and leakage points in the pit, investigate cavities around the foundation pit, and promptly arrange for backfilling and sealing. During the rainstorm, the support system exhibited no damage or displacement, with the maximum horizontal displacement of the piles increasing by approximately 70% compared to pre-disaster conditions, reaching a maximum displacement of 14.58 mm. Based on the rate of change, the warning level is categorized as red; however, the cumulative displacement remains within the designed control limit of 30 mm, indicating that the structural safety of the pile bodies is still manageable. 

Some monitoring points, such as ground settlement, showed significant changes, exceeding their control limits, warranting an orange warning level. Vertical displacements of certain pipelines, attributed to soil loss, pose risks of pipeline incidents. Considerable soil loss behind the piles and between them due to rainwater runoff has created voids, resulting in locally reduced water and soil pressure behind the retaining piles, which presents a risk of local instability in the support system.

Addressing the disaster issues caused by water and sand leakage in deep foundations, exploring the development patterns of excavation leakage disasters and their control methods is crucial for the safety of underground construction projects. This study combines a case of leakage disaster in a deep excavation with a water-rich sandy layer, elucidating the characteristics and triggering causes of excavation water and sand leakage disasters. Utilizing on-site monitoring and numerical simulation methods, it investigates the deformation characteristics of the local leaking excavation and its control measures. The research findings indicate: (1) The development of excavation leakage disasters is a complex multi-field coupled process, possessing certain concealed and sudden characteristics; (2) The horizontal displacement curve of the retaining wall evolves from a "diagonal" shape to a "pot-bottom" shape, then to a shallow "inverted V" shape and a deep "inverted V" shape, with the maximum horizontal displacement of the retaining wall after leakage being 1.29 to 1.44 times that before leakage; (3) The surface settlement curve behind the wall evolves from a "flat spoon-bottom" shape to a "shallow spoon-bottom" shape and then to a "deep spoon-bottom" shape, with the sensitive area of settlement trough ranging from 1.00he to 1.20he (he being the excavation depth). Local leakage causes the surface settlement trough to deepen and widen, with the maximum surface settlement after leakage being 1.16 to 1.65 times that before leakage; (4) The "jumps" in groundwater level fluctuations can serve as precursor signals for assessing excavation leakage disasters; (5) The variation of support axial force adjusts dynamically during the excavation and support process to coordinate deformation development, with minor fluctuations in support axial force occurring during the leakage process. Implementing a combined treatment measure of "grouting + high-pressure jet grouting piles" can effectively tackle the control challenges posed by leakage disasters in water-rich sandy layer excavations. The results of this study provide a reference for theoretical research and control of leakage disasters in excavation engineering.

To investigate the deformation characteristics of deep foundation pits in thick water-rich sand and the characteristics of frequent seepage disasters in soil-nailing walls, a case database for pit deformations and seepage incidents was established for comprehensive statistical analysis. This led to the identification of the deformation patterns of the surrounding structures and the ground surface outside the pit, along with the key influencing factors. Furthermore, a new classification method for seepage disasters in deep foundation pits through soil-nailing walls was proposed, revealing the main inducing factors of the disasters and summarizing effective response measures. The statistical analysis indicates that: (1) the maximum lateral displacement of the wall (δhm) ranges from (0.03% to 0.48%) H (where H is the excavation depth), and the value of δhm is influenced by factors such as corner effects, precipitation patterns, wall toe embedment conditions, and the length of wall exposure; (2) the ground surface settlement profile exhibits a typical trough shape, with a maximum settlement (δvm) of 0.26%He (where He is the final excavation depth) occurring approximately 0.5He from the wall; (3) seepage through the soil-nailing wall can lead to a sudden increase in δhm, primarily due to the increase in the wall-back soil-water interaction caused by dynamic hydraulic effects and improper grouting pressure; (4) most of the ground surface settlement increment (Δδv1) caused by seepage is less than 0.5%H1, with the main affected area extending approximately 2H1 beyond the pit, where H1 is the depth of seepage, and the disturbed area outside the pit exhibits an inverted conical distribution; (5) seepage disasters can be categorized into three types: minor seepage with no soil loss, severe seepage with slight soil loss, and substantial water and sand leakage; (6) adopting a "pre-disaster prevention and post-disaster emergency response" approach can effectively control seepage disasters in soil-nailing walls.

Pressurized groundwater at high levels has always been a major challenge during the excavation of deep foundation pits. If not properly managed, this can lead to serious engineering accidents. Most incidents of groundwater blowout in foundation pit projects stem from improper handling of pressurized water. Based on the blowout incident at the working well of a deep foundation pit for a cross-river tunnel on the north bank of the Yangtze River, and grounded in the basic theory of groundwater seepage, this study utilizes the Visual MODFLOW groundwater seepage simulation software to conduct a two-dimensional seepage simulation of the target pit. From the perspective of external depressurization dewatering, we analyze and study the management of blowout incidents caused by pressurized water. Using the ABAQUS numerical simulation software, we apply a fluid-solid coupling analysis method to simulate and investigate the impact of excavation and dewatering on pit deformation. Through a comparative study of monitoring data and numerical simulation analysis data, the following conclusions were obtained: (1) For deep foundation pits with a bottom-sealing water barrier, when deep pressurized groundwater causes a blowout, implementing external depressurization dewatering measures yields significant effects; (2) The use of a mixed well structure is more effective than conventional dewatering well structures, resulting in higher dewatering efficiency; when the filter pipe diameter is taken as two-thirds of the thickness of the pressurized aquifer, dewatering effects are further enhanced; (3) Generally, the surface settlement value outside the pit is significantly influenced by the excavation of the pit soil and increases with the excavation depth. After external depressurization dewatering, the surface settlement exhibits a sudden change, with the magnitude of change being approximately twice that under normal construction conditions, varying with the amount of depressurization dewatering; (4) The horizontal displacement of the retaining structure gradually increases with the excavation of the pit, reaching a maximum value near the excavation surface; (5) The axial force in the internal supports gradually increases with the progress of pit excavation, and the installation of lower supports reduces the axial force borne by the upper supports. After external depressurization dewatering, there is a significant reduction in the axial force experienced by each layer of support; (6) The vertical deformation of the base soil exhibits upward concave deformation as excavation progresses, with the amount of uplift increasing with excavation depth. After external depressurization dewatering, the uplift amount of the base significantly decreases.

Foundation deformation is a three-dimensional strain phenomenon that occurs in geological materials due to pore volume reduction and particle reorganization under the load of a structure. The core evaluation indicators include the compression modulus under constrained conditions (Es) and the field-determined deformation modulus (Eo), with the difference between the two arising from variations in testing methods that lead to different constrained conditions. In engineering practice, the deformation is calculated using the layer summation method, which assumes that the soil experiences only vertical compression without lateral deformation, and must be combined with parameters such as the compression coefficient (a1-2) obtained from laboratory tests. A historical case is the Shanghai Industrial Exhibition Hall, completed in 1954, which experienced a settlement of 60 cm in its completion year, with a maximum differential settlement of 23.8 cm, thereby demonstrating the hazards of uneven deformation.

Excavation-induced deformation refers to the phenomena resulting from the pressure differential between the inner and outer sides of supporting structures due to the unloading during excavation of a pit, leading to horizontal displacement of the structures and settlement of the surrounding soil or buildings. The primary types of deformation include horizontal displacement of the retaining wall, uplift of the pit bottom, and surface settlement, with horizontal displacement exhibiting a triangular distribution pattern. Flexible walls are prone to develop "inward bulging" deformations, while rigid walls typically manifest "kick-out" modes. Deformation monitoring indicators encompass the displacement of supporting structures, surface settlement values, and deformation rates, while the monitoring methods include traditional techniques such as inclinometers and total stations, as well as digital numerical simulation technologies. Control measures are implemented by increasing the stiffness of the supporting structures, reinforcing the passive zone soil, and optimizing the support system (for instance, using the SMW method for piles). To ensure pit stability, a block construction approach aligned with the principles of temporal and spatial effects is required, alongside the construction of a base slab structure for displacement constraint. Modern technologies employ three-dimensional finite element models and subplastic constitutive models for deformation predictions, integrating intelligent equipment to achieve millimeter-level precision control.

With the continuous improvement of the urban rail transit network, the excavation depth of subway station foundation pits is increasing, the scale of projects is expanding, and the risks involved are gradually rising. This paper, driven by the practical needs of engineering, focuses on the safety evaluation of subway station foundation pits in the soft soil area of Shanghai as the main research objective. By collecting millions of sets of field measurement data, engineering documentation, and construction conditions of subway foundation pits in Shanghai over recent years, a systematic study was conducted on issues such as the reasonable determination of deformation warning values for foundation pits, identification of major risk sources during the foundation pit construction process, and safety assessment of the construction site. The main content includes the following aspects: 

1. Through summarizing existing engineering accidents and comparing currently common risk assessment methods, a preliminary study on risk management for deep foundation pit engineering was conducted, indicating that the fuzzy comprehensive evaluation method is more suitable for assessing the safety of foundation pit projects. 
2. The judgment criteria for warning values of monitoring items such as inclinometer readings of foundation pit walls, settlement of vertical pillars, surrounding buildings, and pipelines were summarized and discussed, proposing a reasonable and effective set of deformation warning values for foundation pits based on the actual situation of Shanghai's subway engineering. 
3. A statistical analysis of incidents in subway foundation pits in Shanghai in recent years categorized common risk sources as follows: (1) over-excavation of foundation pits and untimely support installation (50%); (2) unfavorable dewatering and waterproofing (28%); (3) improper pressure control during high-pressure jet grouting reinforcement (11%), etc. Furthermore, combined with engineering examples, the monitoring data characterization of different risk sources was summarized and categorized, establishing a risk source identification process based on measured data. 
4. Based on actual measurement data, a safety evaluation level and index system for foundation pits was established. A safety evaluation model for deep foundation pits was constructed using the fuzzy comprehensive evaluation method, enabling a quantitative assessment of the safety of the foundation pit itself and its surrounding environment. 
5. A general procedure for assessing the safety level of deep foundation pit construction based on measured data was proposed: identifying abnormal data — judging which risk source exists based on the characterization of abnormal data (qualitative) — using the fuzzy comprehensive evaluation model to perform risk assessment (quantitative) — and combining the trend of monitoring data changes for risk control. Finally, a brief discussion on directions for further work was provided.

The development of urban underground space and the construction of high-rise buildings have resulted in a significant number of deep excavation projects, with increasing scale and depth. Deep excavation works in urban areas are generally situated within dense building clusters, leading to confined construction sites, where some projects have foundations adjacent to existing buildings or structures. In such environments, using conventional design theories (strength control design) and standard construction techniques for deep excavations often fails to meet the requirements for protecting surrounding environments. Although many scholars and engineering professionals have conducted numerous studies on deformation control in excavation design in recent years, there remain several unresolved issues regarding the relationships between the deformation of excavation support structures, the displacement of surrounding strata, and the deformation of nearby buildings during the excavation process. This study investigates deep excavations, support structures, and adjacent buildings as an interrelated system. Utilizing advanced engineering software, FLAC 4.0, this research performed numerical simulation analysis of the entire excavation process under typical geological conditions in the Beijing area. Various support configurations were analyzed under different distances from neighboring buildings, including cantilever support, single-layer anchor bolts and pile combinations, dual-layer anchor bolts and pile combinations, triple-layer anchor bolts and pile combinations, and soil nail support. The analysis focused on the deformation of nearby buildings, the deformation of support structures, and the development of subsidence in the surrounding strata during the deep excavation process. Detailed evaluations were conducted on the impact of anchor bolts and soil nail support on building deformation, leading to the identification of patterns related to the deformation of excavation support structures, strata subsidence, uneven subsidence of adjacent buildings, and absolute subsidence during the excavation process. Key findings provide practical implications for the design and construction of deep excavation projects. Given the current limited research on building deformation adjacent to deep excavations, the results of this study will hold reference value for future numerical simulations and related research.

With the rapid development of the national economy in China, the urbanization process is accelerating, highlighting the increasingly prominent issue of land resource constraints. The construction of deep foundation pit projects has surged during the development of urban underground spaces and high-rise buildings, with scales and depths continuously increasing. Deep foundation pits in urban areas are generally situated within dense clusters of buildings, resulting in narrow construction sites; some projects have foundations that are in close proximity to existing buildings or structures. In such environments, it has become challenging to meet the requirements for protecting the surrounding environment of foundation pits using existing design theories and conventional construction techniques. Although many scholars and engineering professionals have conducted extensive research on deformation control in foundation pit design in recent years, numerous issues remain to be resolved regarding the relationships between the deformation of the foundation pit support structures, the displacement of surrounding strata, and the deformation of neighboring buildings during the excavation process. This study examines the foundation pit, support structures, and adjacent buildings as part of an integrated system. Utilizing the large-scale engineering software FLAC3D 2.0, a numerical simulation analysis of the entire excavation process of a typical deep foundation pit under standard geological conditions in the Beijing area was conducted. The analysis simulated the impact of variations in the stiffness of the underground diaphragm wall, the number of anchor rods, and the horizontal spacing of anchor rods on various factors including horizontal displacement of the pit walls, settlement of the soil behind the walls, horizontal displacement, tilt, and overall settlement of neighboring buildings. The findings discussed the influencing patterns and yielded several conclusions with practical significance for the design and construction of deep foundation pit projects. Furthermore, a method for the optimized design of horizontal spacing of anchor rods was proposed using technical and economic evaluation techniques.
